about.title: About the JLPT
about.html:
The Japanese-Language Proficiency Test (日本語能力試験) is the most widely recognized certification of Japanese ability for non-native speakers. It is held twice a year (July and December) in Japan and many other countries — some overseas sites run only one session per year, so check your local host institution.

Key facts

  • 5 levels: N5 (easiest) → N1 (hardest). You choose your level when you register — there is no need to pass lower levels first.
  • 100% multiple choice, marked on a pencil mark sheet. There is no writing and no speaking section.
  • No penalty for wrong answers. Never leave a blank — guess every unanswered question.
  • Scores are scaled (0–180), not raw percentages, so difficulty differences between sittings are normalized.
  • Certification never expires (though some employers/immigration uses want results from the last 2 years).
  • N2 is the common requirement for professional work in Japanese companies; N1 is expected for roles handling dense documents (legal, medical, security reports, etc.). N3 is a popular résumé milestone while climbing.

The five levels at a glance

LevelCan-do summary≈ Kanji≈ Vocab≈ Study hours*
N5Basic phrases, classroom Japanese, hiragana/katakana + simple kanji~100~800300–450
N4Everyday conversations and simple passages read slowly~300~1,500550–900
N3Daily-life Japanese at near-natural speed; newspaper headlines~650~3,700900–1,500
N2News, general articles, work conversations at natural speed~1,000~6,0001,400–2,500
N1Abstract essays, editorials, fast nuanced speech in varied contexts~2,000~10,0002,200–4,000+

*Cumulative hours from zero, for learners without prior kanji background. Learners with a kanji background (e.g. Chinese) typically need roughly half. The JLPT stopped publishing official vocab/kanji lists in 2010 — all counts are community estimates.

💡 Which level should I take? Take the level where your practice-test scores sit comfortably above the pass mark — not the level you hope to reach by test day. A pass at N3 beats a fail at N2 on a résumé, and the certificate never expires, so you lose nothing by stepping up next session.

scoring.title: Scoring & pass marks
scoring.html:
Every level is scored out of 180 scaled points. To pass you must clear both conditions:

  1. The overall pass mark for your level, and
  2. The sectional minimum in every section. Fail one section and you fail the test, even with a high total.

Pass marks

LevelOverall (of 180)Sections & minimums
N11003 sections × 60 pts:
Language Knowledge (vocab·grammar) / Reading / Listening
≥ 19/60 in each
N290
N395
N4902 scoring sections:
Language Knowledge + Reading 120 pts (≥ 38)
Listening 60 pts (≥ 19)
N580

What this means strategically

  • The overall bar is only ~50–56%. You do not need to be perfect — you need to be consistently okay everywhere.
  • The sectional minimum (19/60 ≈ 32%) exists to stop people passing on grammar alone while bombing listening. Your weakest section sets your risk, so allocate study time to the weakest skill first.
  • Scaled scoring means a brutal sitting won't sink you: getting hard questions right is worth relatively more.
  • Since wrong answers cost nothing, your expected score from pure guessing is ~25% of unanswered points — always fill every bubble before time is called.
⚠ Common failure pattern Strong readers at N2/N1 most often fail on the listening sectional minimum, and self-taught learners most often fail N3+ on reading speed (running out of time, not comprehension). Diagnose with a timed mock test early.

format.title: Test sections & timing
format.html:
The day is split into timed sessions with breaks between them. Current session lengths (revised several times in recent years — confirm on jlpt.jp before your sitting):

LevelSession 1Session 2Listening
N5Vocabulary — 20 minGrammar · Reading — 40 min30 min
N4Vocabulary — 25 minGrammar · Reading — 55 min35 min
N3Vocabulary — 30 minGrammar · Reading — 70 min40 min
N2Language Knowledge · Reading — 105 min (one combined session)50 min
N1Language Knowledge · Reading — 110 min (one combined session)55 min

Question families you will meet

Japanese nameWhat it isLevels
漢字読み / 表記Kanji reading ↔ writing recognitionAll
文脈規定Pick the word that fits the sentence contextAll
言い換え類義Choose the closest paraphrase of the underlined wordAll
用法Pick the sentence that uses the word correctlyN3–N1 (light at N4)
文法形式の判断Pick the correct grammar form for the blankAll
文の組み立て（★）Order 4 fragments; answer = the one on the ★ slotAll — see ★ technique
文章の文法Cloze blanks inside a continuous passageAll
読解Short → long passages, info-retrieval, (N1/N2) compare two textsAll
聴解Task / point / gist comprehension, quick response, integrated comprehensionAll
💡 The mark sheet is part of the test. Answers are numbered 1–4 across all levels. Transfer answers in batches at natural breakpoints, and re-check alignment whenever you skip a question — one misaligned row can wreck a whole section.

roadmap.title: Study roadmap & resources
roadmap.html:
A plan that survives contact with real life

  1. Sit a full mock test first. Past papers and the official 公式問題集 (Official Workbook) tell you your real level and which section drags you down.
  2. Fix the sectional-minimum risk first. 30+ min of listening immersion daily beats a weekend binge; reading speed only grows with daily timed reading.
  3. Drill question formats, not just content. Most "JLPT fails" are format fails: ★ questions, 用法 questions, and listening trap patterns are learnable skills.
  4. The last month is for mocks. One full, timed, mark-sheet mock per week; review every miss the next day.

Battle-tested resources

TypeResourceNotes
Drill series新完全マスター (Shin Kanzen Master)The gold standard for N3–N1, especially grammar & reading. Dense; closest to real exam tone.
Drill series日本語総まとめ (Nihongo Sou Matome)Lighter, week-by-week format. Good first pass; pair with Kanzen for depth.
GrammarTRY! / 文法から伸ばす日本語Grammar in context with listening — good N5–N3 backbone.
TextbookGenki I·II → Quartet / TobiraGenki covers ~N5–N4; Quartet/Tobira bridge to N3–N2.
Vocab/KanjiAnki (Kaishi 1.5k, JLPT Tango decks), WaniKaniDaily SRS is the single highest-ROI habit.
ListeningNihongo no Mori (YouTube), 聴解 Kanzen books, podcasts (Nihongo con Teppei), drama/anime with JP subsNihongo no Mori's free N3–N1 grammar/listening playlists are excellent.
MocksJLPT 公式問題集 vol.1 & 2, ゴカク/模試 collectionsAlways under real timing with a printed mark sheet.
💡 Rough pacing rule. One JLPT level ≈ 6–12 months of consistent (1–2 h/day) study at N5–N3, longer at N2→N1. Registration usually closes 3–4 months before test day — plan backwards from the July/December dates.

universal.title: Universal rules
universal.html:
The five rules that apply at every level

  1. Never leave a blank. No penalty for wrong answers. In the last minute of each section, bubble something for every remaining question — pick one "lucky number" (e.g. always 3) in advance so you don't waste seconds deciding.
  2. First instinct, then move. The test rewards coverage. A question you agonize over for 3 minutes costs you 3–4 easier points later. Mark it lightly on the question paper, answer your best guess, and return only if time remains.
  3. Elimination beats recognition. At N3+ the wrong options are engineered to look right. Striking out two impossible options and choosing between the rest doubles your guessing odds.
  4. Protect the mark sheet. Erase fully, keep bubbles dark, and verify question-number alignment every time you skip. Bring 2–3 sharpened HB pencils and a clean eraser — mechanical pencils are officially discouraged at some sites.
  5. Bring an analog watch. Many rooms have no visible clock and phones must be off and stowed. Smartwatches are banned. Write your "halfway checkpoint" time on scratch space mentally when the section starts.

Logistics that quietly fail people

  • Test voucher + valid photo ID — no voucher, no entry at most sites.
  • Arrive early: doors close before the listening audio check, and late entry to listening is not permitted.
  • No dictionaries, no phones (alarms during the test can void your exam at strict sites), no notes on your desk.
  • Eat between sessions — N2/N1 mornings are long, and listening is last, when you're most tired.
💡 Energy management is a strategy. Listening always comes last. Practice doing mock listening sections after a full reading block, not fresh, so test-day fatigue isn't a surprise.

star.title: The ★ question (文の組み立て)
star.html:
Every level has 4–5 "scrambled sentence" questions: a sentence with four blanks ＿ ＿ ★ ＿ and four fragments to order. You answer only the fragment that lands on the ★. These are the most missed easy points on the exam — and the most learnable.

The 4-step method

  1. Ignore the ★ at first. Your job is to build the whole 4-piece chunk; the star is just where you report from.
  2. Find the forced connections. Grammar patterns lock pieces together: a particle must follow its noun, 〜たことがある needs た-form before こと, 〜ば〜ほど comes as a pair, な-adjectives need な before nouns. Build these 2-piece units first.
  3. Anchor to both ends. The words printed before the first blank and after the last blank constrain the chain. Check what can grammatically attach to each side.
  4. Read the full sentence silently, then report the ★ piece. If it doesn't read naturally end to end, your order is wrong.

Worked example (N4-level)

あした　＿　＿　★　＿　つもりです。
1. 友だち　2. 映画を　3. と　4. 見に行く Forced pairs: 友だち＋と (noun+particle), 見に行く＋つもり (verb plain form before つもり). Chain: 友だち→と→映画を→見に行く. ★ = 3rd slot = 映画を (2).
⚠ The classic trap Most wrong answers come from people who solve the order correctly and then report the first piece instead of the ★ piece. The ★ is usually the 3rd slot but not always — check its position every single time.
💡 If stuck: the piece containing the main grammar point being tested almost never sits on the ★ — the ★ slot tends to hold a connecting noun/object. Use that as a tiebreaker, not a rule.

vocabtactics.title: Vocab question tactics
vocabtactics.html:
漢字読み — kanji reading

  • Wrong options differ by voicing (か/が), vowel length (こ/こう), or っ. Say the word in your head before looking at the options, then match.
  • If you don't know it: compounds are usually on-yomi + on-yomi; single kanji with okurigana are usually kun-yomi. This alone eliminates 1–2 options.

文脈規定 — fill the context

  • Read the whole sentence first; the deciding clue is often after the blank (a verb that collocates, a negative ending).
  • Think in collocations: 約束を守る, 風邪をひく, 迷惑をかける. The test loves verbs that pair with specific nouns.

言い換え類義 — paraphrase

  • Replace the underlined word with each option in the original sentence. The right one preserves meaning and grammar fit.
  • Beware options that are related but shifted in degree or politeness (うれしい vs 楽しい).

用法 — correct usage (N3+)

The hardest vocab format. Four sentences use the target word; only one is natural. Strategy:

  • Fix the word's part of speech, typical subject/object, and register in your mind first. Wrong options usually use the context of a near-synonym (e.g. using 回復 where 復習 belongs).
  • Ask: "Who/what does this word naturally attach to?" 渋滞 attaches to traffic — a sentence about a crowded fridge is out.
💡 Time budget: vocab questions should average 20–30 seconds each. They're worth the same as reading questions that take 3 minutes — bank the time here and spend it on reading.

readingtactics.title: Reading tactics
readingtactics.html:
The core loop

  1. Short passages (1 question): read the question first, then the passage once. The question is almost always the author's main point or a "what does this refer to" — keep that lens on while reading.
  2. Medium/long passages: skim questions first (don't memorize options), read the passage in one pass marking ◯ next to lines that match a question, then answer.
  3. Answer from the text, not from your knowledge. Plausible-in-real-life options that aren't stated in the passage are the #1 trap at N2/N1.

Where Japanese hides the point

  • After the contrast marker. What follows しかし／ところが／だが／とはいえ is usually the author's actual claim; what precedes it is the setup or common view being rejected.
  • At the end. Japanese essays often state the thesis in the final paragraph. For "what does the author want to say" questions, weight the last third heavily.
  • Hedged conclusions are conclusions. 〜のではないだろうか／〜と言えるだろう = the author asserting, softly. Don't dismiss them as uncertainty.
  • Pointer words: for それ／この〜 questions, the referent is almost always in the 1–2 sentences immediately before. Substitute your answer back in to verify.

Special formats

  • 情報検索 (info retrieval — flyers, schedules, notices): do not read the whole notice. Extract the question's conditions (deadline? eligibility? fee?), then scan for exactly those fields. Footnotes (※) contain the answer disturbingly often.
  • Compare-two-texts (N2/N1 統合理解): read A, answer what you can, read B, then handle agree/disagree questions. Track each author's stance with a +/− margin note.
  • NOT questions (合わないもの／正しくないもの): circle the negation in the question stem before reading options — under time pressure people answer the opposite of what was asked.
⚠ Time is the real boss. Most N3–N1 reading failures are pacing failures. Decide in advance: long passages get a hard cap (~8–10 min), and any question over ~90 seconds gets a guess + a mark to revisit.

listentactics.title: Listening tactics
listentactics.html:
Audio plays once. The skill being tested is not just comprehension — it's recovery and focus management.

Per-format strategy

FormatWhat's really askedTactic
課題理解What the person must do next / firstThe conversation lists several tasks; some are already done or assigned to someone else. Track only the listener's remaining task. Listen hard for まず／その前に／じゃ、〜しておいて.
ポイント理解One specific detail (reason, time, opinion)You get ~20 s to read the question and options first — use it. You then listen for the answer instead of holding everything.
概要理解Gist / speaker's main point (no printed options until after)Don't chase details. Ask "what is this person ultimately saying?" — usually crystallized near the end.
発話表現Picture + situation: what would you say?Tests set phrases & keigo direction (who is doing the favor). Drill giving/receiving verbs.
即時応答One line → pick the natural reply (very fast)Answer instantly and reset. Dwelling on Q7 makes you miss Q8 and Q9 — the cascade kills more points than any single hard item. If unsure, bubble and let go.
統合理解 (N2/N1)Long multi-speaker discussion, sometimes 2 questionsTake micro-notes: one keyword per speaker/option (A=price, B=location…). Final question often asks what each of two people chose — track them separately.

The trap patterns to expect

  • The mind-change: the speaker proposes A, hesitates, and settles on B with でも、やっぱり…／そうですね、じゃあ…. The final decision wins. Early options are bait.
  • The done-already: in task questions, items already completed (もう〜ておきました) appear as options.
  • Negative questions & soft refusals: 〜なくてもいい (no need to), ちょっと… (= no), いいです／結構です (often = no thanks).
  • Numbers said twice: a corrected time/price (3時…あ、すみません、4時でした) — the correction wins.
💡 Daily habit that moves the needle: shadowing 5–10 minutes of level-appropriate audio daily trains your ear to the exam's speech rhythm far better than passive watching. In the final month, do listening mocks with cheap earphones or laptop speakers — exam-room audio is never studio quality.

timemgmt.title: Time management budgets
timemgmt.html:
Rough per-question budgets that leave a 5-minute buffer for mark-sheet transfer and review. Adjust to your own mock-test data.

Vocabulary / grammar sessions

Question typeBudget
Kanji reading / orthography15–20 s
Context / paraphrase25–35 s
用法 (usage)45–60 s
Grammar blank30–40 s
★ ordering60–90 s
Passage cloze (文章の文法)~1 min per blank

Reading (the section that decides N2/N1)

Passage typeBudget
Short (1 q)2–3 min total
Medium (2–3 q)5–7 min total
Long (3–4 q)8–11 min total
Compare-two / 統合理解6–8 min
情報検索3–4 min — scan, don't read

Recommended order (N2/N1 combined session)

  1. Vocabulary fast pass (bank time).
  2. Grammar including ★ questions.
  3. 情報検索 next — it's at the back of the booklet but it's the fastest reading points on the test.
  4. Short → medium → long passages. If time collapses, sacrifice one long passage (3 points) — never sacrifice five short questions (5 points).
💡 Write your checkpoints. Before starting, decide e.g. "vocab+grammar done by minute 35, reading from minute 36." A drifting clock is the most common silent failure in the combined N2/N1 session.

n5-overview.title: N5 — Overview & format
n5-overview.html:
N5 certifies basic Japanese: hiragana, katakana, ~100 kanji, ~800 words, and slow, clearly spoken classroom-style conversation. Everything on the paper uses kana with simple kanji — no romaji anywhere, so script fluency is non-negotiable.

Format

SessionTimeContents
Language Knowledge (vocab)20 minKanji reading, orthography (kana↔kanji), context fill, paraphrase
Language Knowledge (grammar) · Reading40 minGrammar blanks, ★ ordering, passage cloze; short passages & one info-retrieval task
Listening30 minTask & point comprehension (with pictures), utterance expressions, quick response

Pass: 80/180 overall · ≥38/120 (LK+Reading) · ≥19/60 (Listening).

What actually gets tested hardest

  • Particles — は・が・を・に・で・へ・と・も・から・まで. Roughly a third of N5 grammar points hinge on particle choice.
  • Time & number readings — dates (ついたち〜とおか, はつか), counters, clock times (4時=よじ, 9時=くじ).
  • て-form mechanics — requests, permission, prohibition, ongoing actions.
  • Katakana words — loanword spelling (コーヒー vs コーヒ) is a guaranteed orthography question.
💡 Pace reality check: the vocab session is only 20 minutes — under 30 seconds per question. Drill until kana reading is automatic; sounding out words letter-by-letter is what makes people run out of time at N5.

n5-grammar.title: N5 — Grammar essentials
n5-grammar.html:
The non-negotiable core

PatternMeaning / useExample
AはBですA is B (topic + copula)わたしは学生です。
〜があります／いますexistence (things / living)つくえの上にねこがいます。
〜てくださいplease doここに名前を書いてください。
〜てもいいですmay / it's okay toここで写真をとってもいいですか。
〜てはいけませんmust not教室でたばこをすってはいけません。
〜ていますongoing action / state今、雨がふっています。
〜たいですwant to (verb)日本へ行きたいです。
〜ましょう／ませんかlet's / won't youいっしょに昼ごはんを食べませんか。
〜たことがありますhave done beforeすしを食べたことがあります。
AよりBのほうが〜B more than A電車よりバスのほうが安いです。
〜ながらwhile doing音楽を聞きながら勉強します。
〜から（reason）because時間がないから、タクシーで行きます。

Particle cheat-sheet (the real exam battlefield)

  • は topic vs が subject/new info: question words take が (だれが来ましたか).
  • に = point in time, destination, existence location; で = location of action, means/tool. 図書館で勉強します／図書館にいます.
  • を = direct object, also "through/along" with motion (公園を散歩します) — a favorite N5 curveball.
  • へ ≈ に for direction; と = with/and; も = also; から〜まで = from〜until.

Conjugation must-knows

  • い-adjectives: 高い→高くない→高かった→高くなかった. ⚠ いい conjugates from よ: よくない・よかった.
  • な-adjectives take な before nouns: きれいな花 — but きれい・ゆうめい look like い-adjectives. Classic trap.
  • て-form groups: う→って, く→いて (⚠行く→行って), ぐ→いで, す→して, ぬ/ぶ/む→んで, る(ichidan)→て.
⚠ Top N5 grammar traps ① きれいくない ✗ → きれいじゃない ✓ ② いいでした ✗ → よかったです ✓ ③ Using に with する-locations: 学校にべんきょうします ✗ → で ✓.

n5-vocab.title: N5 — Vocabulary & kanji
n5-vocab.html:
The ~100 kanji, by theme

  • Numbers & time: 一二三四五六七八九十百千万円年月日時分半週間今
  • People & size: 人男女子父母友先生大小高安新古長
  • Nature & direction: 山川天気雨電花魚犬東西南北上下中外左右前後間
  • Verbs & daily life: 行来出入見聞読書話買食飲休立会言学校駅店道車国社白

Readings the exam loves

ThemeThe hard ones
Days of month一日 ついたち · 二日 ふつか · 三日 みっか … 十日 とおか · 十四日 じゅうよっか · 二十日 はつか
Clock四時 よじ · 七時 しちじ · 九時 くじ — never よん/きゅう here
People counter一人 ひとり · 二人 ふたり · 三人 さんにん
Common counters〜つ (ひとつ…とお), 〜枚 flat, 〜本 long (一本 いっぽん・三本 さんぼん・六本 ろっぽん), 〜匹 small animals, 〜冊 books
Familymy family (humble): 父・母・兄・姉 vs someone else's: お父さん・お母さん・お兄さん・お姉さん — tested every year

Katakana orthography

One question set checks loanword spelling. Drill long-vowel marks and small kana: コーヒー・パーティー・シャワー・エレベーター・コンピューター・ニュース.

💡 Highest-ROI drill: number/date/counter listening. The N5 listening section asks for times, dates, prices, and quantities constantly — being instant on じゅっぷん vs じっぷん, ろっぴゃく, はっせん is worth several points.

n5-reading.title: N5 — Reading strategy
n5-reading.html:
N5 reading is short: a few 1–2 paragraph passages and one notice/schedule task. The challenge is kana speed, not interpretation.

How to attack it

  • Read the question first — N5 questions are concrete (When? Where? Who? What did ___ do?). Then scan for that exact detail.
  • Time/sequence words are usually the key: 〜まえに・〜あとで・それから・はじめに.
  • For the notice task (情報検索): match the question's condition (day, price, time) to the table cell. The ※ footnote often flips the obvious answer (e.g. "closed on holidays").
  • Negatives flip answers: 〜ないでください／〜てはいけません in a notice means a "can I…?" question is answered no.

Example micro-passage logic

きのうは雨でしたから、サッカーをしませんでした。うちで本を読みました。 Q: きのう、何をしましたか → The から-clause is the reason; the action that happened is after it. Answer: read a book at home — not soccer (didn't happen), not "it rained" (not an action).
💡 Pacing: the grammar+reading session is 40 minutes. Aim to finish grammar in ~20 so reading is unhurried — most N5 takers who fail this section simply never reached the last passage.

n5-listening.title: N5 — Listening strategy
n5-listening.html:
Speech is slow and scripted, with pictures for many questions. The points are won on numbers, dates, times, locations, and set phrases.

Formats

  • 課題理解 — what should the person do? Pictures show options. Listen for the final instruction.
  • ポイント理解 — one detail (what time? which day?). Numbers are often said twice with a correction — the correction wins.
  • 発話表現 — picture + "what do you say here?" Drill set phrases: いただきます・おさきにしつれいします・いってきます・おだいじに・いらっしゃいませ.
  • 即時応答 — quick response. Common pairs: ありがとう→どういたしまして, すみません、ちょっと… (= refusal), 〜ましょうか→おねがいします.

Trap awareness (yes, even at N5)

  • Position words flip pictures: 上・下・中・前・うしろ・となり・右・左.
  • 〜じゃなくて = "not A, B." The first item named is bait.
  • Mind the politeness direction: どうぞ (offering) vs どうも (thanking).
💡 Drill source: the official 公式問題集 audio + any N5 listening playlist at 1.0× — then re-listen at 1.25× in the final weeks so real exam speed feels slow.

n5-tips.title: N5 — Tips & traps
n5-tips.html:
Score-moving tips

  1. Kana speed is the test. If you read hiragana at half speed, every section suffers. Daily reading aloud for 10 minutes fixes this faster than any drill book.
  2. Memorize exception readings as words, not rules: 今日 きょう・明日 あした・大人 おとな・一人 ひとり・上手 じょうず・下手 へた.
  3. Don't translate to English in your head during listening — at N5 speed you can keep up only if you picture the scene directly.
  4. The ★ questions at N5 are nearly free points with the 4-step method — particles lock almost everything in place.

Trap gallery

⚠ Look-alike kana & kanji シ/ツ, ソ/ン, め/ぬ, わ/れ — and kanji pairs 右/石, 千/午, 体/休, 白/百. Orthography questions are built on exactly these.
⚠ Voicing & small tsu in readings がっこう not がこう; ざっし not ざし; きって not きて. If two options differ only by っ or ten-ten, slow down — that IS the question.
⚠ こ・そ・あ・ど これ/それ/あれ + ここ/そこ/あそこ mapping to speaker/listener/neither — a guaranteed grammar or listening point.

n4-overview.title: N4 — Overview & format
n4-overview.html:
N4 certifies everyday Japanese: ~300 kanji, ~1,500 words, and conversations on daily topics spoken slowly. It is where Japanese grammar's machinery — potential, passive, giving/receiving, conditionals — first appears in force.

Format

SessionTimeContents
Language Knowledge (vocab)25 minKanji reading, orthography, context, paraphrase, (light) usage
Grammar · Reading55 minGrammar blanks, ★ ordering, passage cloze; short & medium passages, info retrieval
Listening35 minTask / point comprehension, utterance expressions, quick response

Pass: 90/180 overall · ≥38/120 (LK+Reading) · ≥19/60 (Listening).

The N4 difficulty spikes

  • Transitive/intransitive pairs (開ける/開く, 消す/消える…) — the single most reliable N4 question topic.
  • Giving & receiving (あげる・くれる・もらう and their て-form combos) — direction-of-favor logic.
  • Four conditionals (と・ば・たら・なら) with overlapping but distinct rules.
  • Plain form everywhere — reported speech (〜と思う/〜と言う), noun-modifying clauses.
💡 N4 is a grammar exam wearing a vocab costume. If your mock scores lag, it's almost always the grammar section — prioritize TRY! N4 or Sou Matome grammar over more vocab cramming.

n4-grammar.title: N4 — Grammar essentials
n4-grammar.html:
The big machinery

PatternUseExample
potential 〜(ら)れる／〜えるcan do漢字が読めるようになりました。
passive 〜(ら)れるis done (often "suffering" passive)電車で足をふまれました。
causative 〜(さ)せるmake/let someone do母は弟に野菜を食べさせました。
〜なければなりません／〜なくてはいけませんmust明日までにレポートを出さなければなりません。
〜なくてもいいですneed not靴をぬがなくてもいいです。
〜ておくdo in advance会議の前に資料を読んでおきます。
〜てしまうcompletely / regrettablyさいふを忘れてしまいました。
〜てみるtry doing新しい店に行ってみたいです。
〜そうです (looks)looks like (stem+そう)このケーキはおいしそうです。
〜そうです (hearsay)I hear that (plain+そう)田中さんは来月結婚するそうです。
〜ようです／〜らしいですseems / apparently外は雨が降っているようです。
〜ように／〜ことにする・なるso that / decide / turn out毎朝走ることにしました。

Giving & receiving — the direction game

  • あげる: I/someone gives outward. くれる: someone gives to me/my group. もらう: receive (giver marked with に/から).
  • て-form versions describe favors: 手伝ってくれた (they kindly helped me), 手伝ってもらった (I had them help), 手伝ってあげた (I helped them).
先生が私に本を貸してくださいました。＝ 私は先生に本を貸していただきました。Same event, two viewpoints — paraphrase questions love this equivalence.

The four conditionals in one box

  • と — automatic/natural result: 春になると、花がさきます。 (no requests/commands after と)
  • ば — general "if": 安ければ買います。
  • たら — the all-rounder, incl. one-time and "when…then discovered": 家に帰ったら、手紙が来ていた。
  • なら — responding to topic/context: 日本に行くなら、京都がいいですよ。
⚠ Exam favorites ① と + command is ungrammatical — eliminate it on sight. ② Potential verbs take が: 日本語が話せます. ③ Don't stack potential on する→できる (しられる ✗).

n4-vocab.title: N4 — Vocabulary & kanji
n4-vocab.html:
Kanji jump: ~100 → ~300

New kanji cluster around daily life: 家族・仕事・勉強・旅行・病院・薬・銀行・地図・世界・特別・運転・注意・用意・経験… On-yomi compounds become the norm, so the reading section starts testing which on-yomi (生: せい/しょう; 楽: がく/らく).

Transitive / intransitive pairs — memorize as pairs

Transitive (someone does)Intransitive (happens)
開ける／閉める開く／閉まる
つける／消すつく／消える
始める／止める始まる／止まる
入れる／出す入る／出る
壊す／落とす壊れる／落ちる
直す／並べる直る／並ぶ

Particle logic: transitive takes を, intransitive takes が. ドアが開いています (state) vs ドアを開けています (someone's action) — and 〜ている on intransitives means resulting state, a classic question.

Adverbs & function words the test recycles

やっと・ぜひ・きっと・たぶん・なかなか（＋negative）・ほとんど・だいぶ・そろそろ・とうとう・もうすぐ・ちっとも（＋negative）

💡 Paraphrase prep: N4 言い換え questions favor these swaps — うるさい≒静かにしてほしい, るす≒家にいない, したく≒準備, 用事がある≒忙しい, びっくりする≒驚く. Study synonyms in pairs, not isolated lists.

n4-reading.title: N4 — Reading strategy
n4-reading.html:
Passages grow to 2–4 paragraphs (emails, diary entries, simple explanations) plus an info-retrieval notice. New challenge: questions about reasons and references, not just facts.

Question patterns & counters

  • どうして〜ますか — locate the から/ので/ため clause nearest the event. The reason stated in the text wins over the plausible real-world reason.
  • 「それ」「この〜」 reference questions — answer is in the previous 1–2 sentences. Substitute back to confirm.
  • Writer's feeling — emotion words are explicit at N4 (うれしかった・ざんねんだった・びっくりした); find the sentence containing them.
  • Sequence questions — chart the time words: まず→それから→そのあと→さいごに.

Info retrieval at N4

Notices add conditions: age limits, "bring ◯◯", different prices per day. Method: underline every condition in the question, then check the table + the ※ notes. Two conditions usually intersect at exactly one option.

💡 Budget: 55 minutes covers grammar AND reading. Checkpoint: grammar done by minute 25. The medium passage is worth multiple points — never let ★ questions eat its time.

n4-listening.title: N4 — Listening strategy
n4-listening.html:
Speed increases slightly, pictures decrease, and conversations gain one twist each — a changed plan, a condition, a refusal.

What to train

  • Task comprehension: several tasks are mentioned; some are done (もう〜ました), some are someone else's. Answer = the listener's next undone task. Listen for じゃあ、まず／さきに.
  • Plan changes: 〜つもりだったけど／雨だから、やっぱり… The post-けど/やっぱり content is the answer.
  • Permission/refusal softeners: ちょっと…・すみません、その日は…・〜はちょっと難しいですね = no.
  • Quick response set phrases: おかげさまで・かしこまりました・おまたせしました・〜たらどうですか→そうですね、そうします.

Listening for grammar

N4 listening quietly tests grammar by ear: potential (行けない = can't go ≠ won't go), 〜てしまった (regret), 〜なくてもいい (don't have to — so the person doesn't do it). If these still need mental translation, drill them specifically with audio.

💡 Numbers round 2: N4 loves prices with discounts (2割引・半額), durations (〜時間半), and date changes. Practice writing down only the FINAL stated number.

n4-tips.title: N4 — Tips & traps
n4-tips.html:
Score-moving tips

  1. Own the transitivity pairs. They appear in vocab, grammar, AND listening. One afternoon making a pair table pays off three times.
  2. Plain form fluency. ★ questions and noun-modifying clauses (きのう買った本) assume instant plain-form conversion. Drill ます↔plain both directions.
  3. Learn こ・そ・あ at discourse level: その〜 referring back in text is an every-exam reading question.
  4. Don't over-study kanji writing — the exam only tests recognition. Reading speed > writing ability.

Trap gallery

⚠ Look-alike grammar 〜そうだ looks vs hearsay (stem+そう vs plain+そう): 降りそうだ (about to rain) ≠ 降るそうだ (I heard it'll rain). The blank before そう IS the question.
⚠ あげる／くれる direction If the receiver is "me/my side," it must be くれる. 友だちが私に教えてあげた ✗.
⚠ Potential vs passive Both can be 〜られる. Disambiguate by particles: potential pairs with が + thing; passive has an agent marked with に.

n3-overview.title: N3 — Overview & format
n3-overview.html:
N3 is the bridge level: ~650 kanji, ~3,700 words, near-natural-speed conversation, and the first appearance of real essays. It also switches to three separately-scored sections, so listening alone can now fail you.

Format

SessionTimeContents
Language Knowledge (vocab)30 minKanji reading, orthography, context, paraphrase, usage (用法)
Grammar · Reading70 minGrammar blanks, ★, passage cloze; short/medium/long passages, info retrieval
Listening40 minTask, point, gist (概要理解), utterance, quick response

Pass: 95/180 — note this is higher than N2's 90 — with ≥19/60 in each of the three sections.

What makes N3 feel like a wall

  • Volume: vocab roughly 2.5× N4. SRS (Anki/WaniKani) stops being optional.
  • 用法 questions debut seriously — knowing a word's meaning isn't enough; you need its natural habitat.
  • Gist listening (概要理解) has no printed options while you listen — pure comprehension.
  • Long passages mean pacing strategy now decides outcomes. See time budgets.
💡 The N3 secret: it borrows heavily from N4 grammar in harder clothing. Reviewing N4 patterns until they're reflexive raises N3 scores more than racing through new N3 lists.

n3-grammar.title: N3 — Grammar essentials
n3-grammar.html:
High-frequency patterns

PatternMeaningExample
〜はずだ／〜はずがないshould be (expectation) / can't be彼はもう着いているはずだ。
〜わけだ／〜わけではないno wonder; that means / it's not that…嫌いなわけではないが、あまり食べない。
〜うちにwhile (before the chance passes)熱いうちに食べてください。
〜ばかりjust did / nothing but日本に来たばかりです。ゲームばかりしている。
〜ところだabout to / in the middle of / just did今、出かけるところです。
〜ように／〜ためにso that (non-volitional) / in order to (volitional)聞こえるように大きい声で話す。／合格するために勉強する。
〜させられるcausative-passive (made to do)部長に残業させられた。
〜ても／〜のにeven if / despite (with frustration)何度読んでもわからない。練習したのに負けた。
〜たびに・〜おかげで・〜せいでevery time / thanks to / blame on寝坊したせいで遅刻した。
〜ことになる／〜ことにするit's been decided / I decide大阪に転勤することになりました。
〜つもりだったのに・〜ようとするintended but… / try to電話しようとしたら、向こうからかかってきた。
〜らしい・〜っぽい・〜みたいだtypical of / -ish / seems彼は男らしい。子どもっぽい服。

The はず／わけ／ところ cluster

N3's signature confusables. Anchor them:

  • はず = logical expectation from evidence ("the schedule says so").
  • わけだ = conclusion clicking into place ("ah, that's why"). わけではない = partial negation; わけにはいかない (more N2) = can't, for social/moral reasons.
  • ところ snapshots an action: するところ (about to) / しているところ (mid) / したところ (just finished).

Keigo enters the exam

Recognize the big irregulars both ways: いらっしゃる（行く/来る/いる）・おっしゃる（言う）・召し上がる（食べる）・ご覧になる（見る）・なさる（する） vs humble 伺う・申す・いただく・拝見する・いたす. N3 tests recognition; production mastery can wait for N2.

⚠ ように／ために The exam's favorite: ように follows potential/non-volitional verbs (読めるように), ために follows volitional (買うために). Check the verb type before the blank — it usually decides instantly.

n3-vocab.title: N3 — Vocabulary & kanji
n3-vocab.html:
Kanji jump: ~300 → ~650

Abstract and social vocabulary arrives: 関係・経済・政治・情報・約束・想像・態度・性格・原因・結果・確認・依頼・募集・遅刻. Multi-reading kanji become a question category in themselves (直: ちょく/じき/なお; 代: だい/たい/か/しろ).

Word families to study as sets

  • する-nouns: 準備・用意・支度 (all "prepare" — different naturalness per context: 用意 things, 準備 broad, 支度 personal/going out). This trio is a perennial 用法 question.
  • Emotion adjectives: 恥ずかしい・悔しい・懐かしい・羨ましい・恐ろしい — paraphrase bait.
  • Adverb + negative sets: めったに〜ない・必ずしも〜ない・少しも〜ない・別に〜ない.
  • Onomatopoeia debut: ぐっすり（寝る）・がっかり（する）・うっかり（忘れる）・しっかり・どきどき・いらいら — they collocate with specific verbs; learn the pairs.

用法 survival method

「募集」— ✓ アルバイトを募集している。 ✗ 友だちを募集して遊んだ。 募集 = formally recruiting applicants. The wrong options put the word in a casual context where 誘う belongs. Always ask: what register, what typical object?
💡 SRS settings that work at N3 volume: ~15–20 new words/day with sentence cards (word in context), not isolated word→meaning cards. Context cards double as 文脈規定 practice.

n3-reading.title: N3 — Reading strategy
n3-reading.html:
Essays, columns, and explanatory texts appear, including opinion pieces where the author disagrees with a common view — the classic JLPT essay shape.

The N3 essay skeleton (learn it once, use it forever)

  1. Common belief: 一般的に〜と言われている／〜と思っている人が多い
  2. Pivot: しかし／だが／本当にそうだろうか
  3. Author's claim (the answer to "main point" questions lives here and in the conclusion)
  4. Example/evidence: 例えば
  5. Restated conclusion: つまり／このように／〜のではないだろうか

Tactics

  • Underline pivots (しかし系) and conclusion markers (つまり系) as you read — main-point questions are then 30-second lookups.
  • 下線部 questions ("what does the underlined part mean / why"): the explanation is within ±2 sentences of the underline, usually right after.
  • Option elimination at N3: wrong options are (a) true but not asked, (b) too strong (絶対・全部・必ず when the text hedges), or (c) the rejected common view from paragraph 1.
  • Long passage cap: 10 minutes. Budget table →
💡 Daily reading that matches N3: NHK News Web Easy, graded readers level 3–4, and Sou Matome N3 reading. Read with a timer from day one — untimed reading builds comprehension but not exam survival.

n3-listening.title: N3 — Listening strategy
n3-listening.html:
Natural-ish speed, contracted forms, and the debut of 概要理解 (gist) — you hear a short monologue (announcement, speech, explanation) and only THEN hear the question and options.

Gist questions without panic

  • While listening, keep one running summary sentence in your head: "This is a person explaining ___ because ___."
  • The question is usually "何について話していますか" or "一番言いたいことは何ですか" — your summary sentence answers both.
  • Don't note details; gist questions never ask them.

Contracted speech — learn to hear these

You'll hearIt means
〜ちゃう／じゃう〜てしまう
〜とく〜ておく
〜てる／〜でる〜ている
〜なきゃ／〜なくちゃ〜なければならない
〜って〜と（言う）quotation

Trap escalation

  • Two-condition answers: "Saturday if it doesn't rain, otherwise Sunday" — and the question asks the rainy case.
  • Opinion vs fact: the speaker reports others' views (〜と言う人もいますが) before giving their own (私は〜と思います). The 私は clause wins.
💡 Train with monologues, not just dialogues: news clips, vlog intros, store announcements. 概要理解 is monologue-shaped, and most learners only practice conversations.

n3-tips.title: N3 — Tips & traps
n3-tips.html:
Score-moving tips

  1. Respect the 95 pass mark. N3 requires a higher total than N2. Aim for one strong section (usually vocab/grammar) to subsidize your weakest.
  2. Reading speed is built, not crammed: 20 timed minutes daily for 3 months beats a final-month panic. Track words-per-minute monthly.
  3. Keigo recognition is cheap points — a weekend with the irregular honorific/humble table covers every keigo question N3 will ask.
  4. 用法 questions: triage. If two options both feel fine after 60 seconds, pick the more "textbook-typical" context and move on.

Trap gallery

⚠ Partial negation family わけではない (not necessarily), とは限らない (not always), ないことはない (it's not that I don't…). These appear in reading options to soften/flip claims — match the hedge level between text and option.
⚠ ばかり's three faces 食べたばかり (just ate) / 食べてばかり (does nothing but eat) / 食べるばかり (all that's left is to eat). The conjugation before ばかり decides.
⚠ Intonation-free sarcasm doesn't exist — but rhetorical questions do 〜のではないだろうか in essays = assertion, not doubt. Options saying the author "isn't sure" are bait.

n2-overview.title: N2 — Overview & format
n2-overview.html:
N2 certifies working-proficiency Japanese: ~1,000 kanji, ~6,000 words, news and general articles, natural-speed conversation. It's the standard bar for jobs in Japanese companies — and the level where the combined 105-minute session makes time management half the battle.

Format

SessionTimeContents
Language Knowledge · Reading (combined)105 minVocab (incl. word formation 語形成), grammar, ★, passage cloze; short→long passages, compare-two-texts, thematic long read, info retrieval
Listening50 minTask, point, gist, quick response, integrated comprehension (統合理解)

Pass: 90/180 · ≥19/60 in each of Language Knowledge / Reading / Listening (scored separately even though the session is combined).

What's genuinely new at N2

  • Written-register grammar: 〜における・〜に伴い・〜をめぐって — forms you read but rarely say.
  • 語形成: prefix/suffix questions (再〜・諸〜・〜率・〜沿い・〜気味).
  • 統合理解 listening: 3 speakers comparing options, sometimes two questions per audio.
  • Volume pressure: ~75 reading-session questions in 105 minutes.
💡 The N2 meta: most failers know enough Japanese — they run out of time or drop below 19 in listening. Build your prep around timed mocks and daily audio from month one, not as a final-month garnish.

n2-grammar.title: N2 — Grammar essentials
n2-grammar.html:
High-yield patterns

PatternMeaningExample
〜わけにはいかないcan't (social/moral duty)明日試験だから、遊んでいるわけにはいかない。
〜ざるを得ないhave no choice but (⚠ する→せざる)台風で、中止せざるを得ない。
〜かねる／〜かねないcan't quite (polite) / might well (bad)その質問にはお答えしかねます。事故になりかねない。
〜にしては／〜わりにfor a… (contrary to expectation)初めてにしては上手だ。値段のわりにおいしい。
〜に限らず・〜に限ってnot only / precisely when (ironic)急いでいる日に限ってバスが来ない。
〜上で／〜上にupon (after) / on top ofよく考えた上で決めた。雨の上に風も強い。
〜ものだ familygeneral truth / nostalgia / exclamation / should子どもはよく寝るものだ。昔はよく遊んだものだ。
〜どころか／〜どころではないfar from / no time for忙しくて旅行どころではない。
〜つつ／〜つつあるwhile; despite knowing / in the process of悪いと知りつつ、嘘をついた。回復しつつある。
〜に伴い・〜とともに・〜につれてalong with / as ~ changes経済の発展に伴い、生活も変わった。
〜をめぐって・〜に関して・〜におけるconcerning / regarding / in (formal)移転をめぐって議論が続いている。
〜得る／〜得ないpossible / impossibleそれはあり得ない。

Distinction pairs the exam farms every year

  • 〜ば〜ほど vs 〜につれて: ほど needs the doubled verb; につれて takes gradual change directly.
  • おかげで (credit) vs せいで (blame) vs ために (neutral cause/purpose) — match the sentence's emotional polarity.
  • 〜がたい (emotionally hard: 信じがたい) vs 〜にくい (physically hard) vs 〜づらい (hard, with discomfort).
  • 〜向け (designed for) vs 〜向き (suited for).
  • 〜際に/〜にあたって (formal "when/on the occasion") vs plain とき — register questions.

Keigo at production level

N2 expects you to pick correct keigo, not just recognize it: お＋stem＋する (humble), お＋stem＋になる (honorific), and to catch wrong-direction keigo in options (honoring yourself = wrong: 私が召し上がります ✗).

⚠ Form-attachment is half the question Many N2 items test the conjugation BEFORE the pattern: ざるを得ない takes ない-stem (せざる), 〜得る attaches to stem, 〜つつ takes stem. If an option's attachment is wrong, the meaning doesn't matter — eliminate.

n2-vocab.title: N2 — Vocabulary & kanji
n2-vocab.html:
Kanji jump: ~650 → ~1,000

News vocabulary dominates: 影響・状況・対策・課題・削減・需要・供給・傾向・誤解・把握・検討・避難・採用・promotion系（昇進・出世）. Expect homophone discrimination (保証/保障/補償 all ほしょう) in orthography questions.

語形成 — word formation (N2-only question type)

AffixMeaningExamples
再〜re-再利用・再開・再確認
非〜／不〜／無〜／未〜negation family非常識・不可能・無関係・未完成 — which negative attaches to which base is the question
〜率／〜費／〜量rate / cost / amount出席率・交通費・生産量
〜気味／〜っぽい／〜げslightly / -ish / looking風邪気味・忘れっぽい・悲しげ
〜沿い／〜向け／〜だらけalong / for / full of (bad)川沿い・子ども向け・泥だらけ

Collocations & idioms that recur

気を遣う・気が済む・腹が立つ・手間がかかる・面倒を見る・身につける・目を通す・耳にする・口にする・話がつく・きりがない

Adverb nuance sets

あいにく（unfortunately, timing）・いまだに（still, surprisingly）・かえって（contrary to intent）・さすがに（as expected/even so）・とりあえず（for now）・改めて（anew/formally）・思い切って（boldly）

💡 Homophone drill: make a list of ほしょう・いどう（移動/異動）・かいとう（回答/解答）・たいしょう（対象/対照/対称）pairs. Orthography questions at N2 are basically this list.

n2-reading.title: N2 — Reading strategy
n2-reading.html:
The reading load: short ×5, medium ×3 passages (≈500 chars), compare-two-texts, one long essay (≈900 chars), and info retrieval — all inside the combined 105-minute session.

Session game plan

  1. Vocab + grammar in ≤35 minutes (they're ⅓ of points but should take ⅓ of time MAX).
  2. Jump to 情報検索 (last pages) — 2 fast points.
  3. Short passages → compare-two → medium → long. The long essay last, with whatever remains (min. 10 minutes).

Compare-two-texts (統合理解) method

  • Read text A; in the margin write its stance in 3–4 words. Same for B.
  • Typical questions: "what do A and B agree on?" / "how do they differ?" Agreement is usually on the problem's existence; difference is on the cause or solution.
  • Watch hedges: A says "should consider", B says "must" — options that swap strength are wrong.

Long essay: claim-hunting

  • The thesis hides behind つまり・要するに・このように・〜のではないか・〜べきだ・〜必要がある — usually final paragraph.
  • "筆者の考えに合うもの" = match the claim, not an example. Options quoting a vivid example from mid-text are classic decoys.
  • Strong-word elimination still works: text hedges → option with 必ず/すべて is out.
⚠ Do not read linearly when behind. If you hit minute 70 with the long essay + medium passages left, switch: questions first, targeted scanning. Partial-credit scanning beats running out of time with perfect comprehension of half the section.

n2-listening.title: N2 — Listening strategy
n2-listening.html:
Natural speed, workplace contexts (instructions from 部長, client calls), and 統合理解: long audio with 3 speakers or 1 explanation + 2 reactors, occasionally with two questions for one audio.

統合理解 note system (practice this exact ritual)

  • Audio describing 4 options (plans, products, courses): write 1–4 down the margin, one keyword each (①安い ②駅近 ③夜OK ④初心者).
  • Then two people discuss: track each person's priority (M=安さ F=時間). Final questions: "What did the man choose? The woman?" — your two-line note answers both.

Workplace listening cues

  • Indirect orders: 〜てもらえるかな／〜ておいてくれる？／お願いできる？ = do it.
  • Soft rejections in business: 検討します・難しいですね・また連絡します often = no.
  • Keigo by ear: identify who's senior from verb forms alone — questions ask "who will do X?" and keigo direction is the only clue.

Quick response at N2 speed

Stimulus lines use idioms and keigo: 「お先に失礼します」→「お疲れさまでした」, 「つまらないものですが」→「ご丁寧にありがとうございます」. Drill 100+ pairs; this sub-section is pure pattern recognition and protects your 19/60 floor.

💡 Immersion that maps to N2: NHK news (normal, not Easy), business-setting dramas, podcasts at natural speed. In the last month add 1.25× speed so exam audio feels comfortable.

n2-tips.title: N2 — Tips & traps
n2-tips.html:
Score-moving tips

  1. Time-box religiously. Write your three checkpoints (vocab/grammar by :35, short+compare by :65, everything by :100) and obey them. The combined session punishes drift more than ignorance.
  2. Grammar form-attachment table. One page listing what each N2 pattern attaches to (stem/ない-stem/plain/noun+の). It converts ~⅓ of grammar questions into instant eliminations.
  3. Read editorials weekly (天声人語-style columns) — N2 long essays are this exact genre.
  4. Listening floor first: if mocks put listening near 19/60, every study day starts with 30 minutes of audio until that's fixed. The sectional minimum is the most common N2 failure.

Trap gallery

⚠ かねる／かねない polarity flip しかねる = can't do (polite refusal) but しかねない = might well do (something bad). Positive-looking form is negative, negative-looking form is positive. The exam exploits this asymmetry constantly.
⚠ ものだ's four jobs general truth / nostalgia (〜たものだ) / strong emotion / mild obligation (〜ものではない = shouldn't). Identify which job fits the sentence before judging options.
⚠ Honorific direction Options that apply 尊敬語 to the speaker's own actions, or 謙譲語 to the listener's, are wrong regardless of how polite they sound. Check WHO acts before checking the form.

n1-overview.title: N1 — Overview & format
n1-overview.html:
N1 certifies comprehension of Japanese in a broad range of circumstances: abstract essays, editorials, dense logic, fast nuanced speech with varied speakers. ~2,000 kanji, ~10,000 words — but the real exam currency is register sensitivity and reading stamina.

Format

SessionTimeContents
Language Knowledge · Reading (combined)110 minVocab, grammar, ★, passage cloze; short→long passages, compare-two, thematic long essay (~1000 chars), info retrieval
Listening55 minTask, point, gist, quick response, integrated comprehension (incl. 2-question audio)

Pass: 100/180 — the highest bar — with ≥19/60 in each section.

How N1 actually differs from N2

  • Grammar: shifts to literary/formal forms (〜んばかり, 〜まじき, 〜ゆえに) plus fine nuance between near-synonymous patterns.
  • Vocab: nuance over breadth — 慣用句 (idioms), 四字熟語, words whose meaning shifts with context.
  • Reading: abstraction. Authors argue about arguments; the question is the author's stance toward an idea, not facts.
  • Listening: speed + implicature. What speakers mean rather than say; opinions wrapped in hedges and irony.
💡 Realistic calibration: pass rates hover around 30–35%. A 100/180 target means you can be mediocre in one section if solid elsewhere — many people pass with ~25/60 reading and 38+ in the other two. Build your personal arithmetic from mock data and play to it.

n1-grammar.title: N1 — Grammar essentials
n1-grammar.html:
Literary & formal heavy-hitters

PatternMeaningExample
〜んばかり(に)as if about to泣かんばかりの顔で頼まれた。
〜んがため(に)in order to (archaic, strong purpose)勝たんがために猛練習した。
〜ともなると／〜ともなればwhen it comes to (that level)プロともなると、責任が違う。
〜を禁じ得ないcan't suppress (emotion)涙を禁じ得なかった。
〜を余儀なくされるbe forced to大雨で中止を余儀なくされた。
〜まじきunbefitting (after あるorする)教師にあるまじき行為だ。
〜（が）ゆえにbecause (formal/written)貧しさゆえに進学を諦めた。
〜の極み／〜の至りthe height of光栄の至りです。贅沢の極みだ。
〜ずにはおかない／〜ないではおかないinevitably causes; won't rest until読む者を感動させずにはおかない作品。
〜を皮切りにstarting with東京を皮切りに全国ツアーを行う。
〜たりとも〜ないnot even one一秒たりとも無駄にできない。
〜であれ／〜だろうとwhether it be / no matter理由が何であれ、暴力は許されない。
〜きらいがあるhas a (bad) tendency彼は物事を悲観的に考えるきらいがある。
〜限りだutterly (emotion)お会いできて嬉しい限りです。

Nuance clusters — where N1 points are actually decided

  • "can't help" family: 〜ずにはいられない (urge from within) / 〜ざるを得ない (forced by circumstance) / 〜ないわけにはいかない (social duty) / 〜を禁じ得ない (emotion, written).
  • "as soon as" family: 〜なり・〜や否や・〜が早いか・〜そばから — そばから implies recurring annoyance; the others are one-time.
  • "because" register ladder: から → ので → ため → ゆえに／〜ことから／〜とあって (とあって = because of a special situation, with observed result).
  • Concession: 〜とはいえ・〜といえども・〜ながらも・〜つつも — all "although," differing in formality and what follows.
⚠ Attachment rules ARE the question 〜んばかり/〜んがため take ない-stem＋ん (する→せんがため); や否や takes dictionary form; まじき follows ある/する. An option with the right meaning but wrong attachment is wrong — and N1 builds wrong options exactly this way.

n1-vocab.title: N1 — Vocabulary & kanji
n1-vocab.html:
What the vocab section really tests

  • Nuance between near-synonyms: 妥協/譲歩, 抱負/野心, 配慮/考慮, 是正/改善, 懸念/不安 — 文脈規定 options are these quadruples.
  • Sound-symbolism precision: ひっそり・こっそり・うんざり・げっそり・ことごとく・まんまと・てっきり（＋思い込み）・かろうじて.
  • 慣用句 (idioms): 手を打つ・足が出る・腑に落ちない・釘を刺す・しのぎを削る・お茶を濁す・拍車をかける・歯が立たない・目から鱗.
  • 四字熟語 the exam reuses: 試行錯誤・一石二鳥・臨機応変・半信半疑・優柔不断・無我夢中・自業自得.
  • Formal verbs of writing: 講じる（対策を）・呈する（疑問を）・余儀なくする・踏まえる・鑑みる・即する.

Kanji at N1

Reading questions favor unusual readings of common kanji over rare kanji: 滞る（とどこおる）・潤う（うるおう）・賄う（まかなう）・廃れる（すたれる）・遮る（さえぎる）・覆す（くつがえす）・憤り（いきどおり）. Drill kun-yomi of N1 verbs specifically.

用法 at N1 — the register test

「殺到」— ✓ 問い合わせが殺到した。 ✗ 朝ご飯に友だちが殺到した。 殺到 demands a large-scale rush toward a target (orders, applications, calls). Wrong options scale it down to casual scenes. At N1, ask: scale? register? typical subject?
💡 Source your vocab from reading, not lists alone: editorials, 新書 paperbacks, long-form journalism. N1 文脈規定 reproduces written-register collocations; learners who only grind decks recognize words but misjudge fit.

n1-reading.title: N1 — Reading strategy
n1-reading.html:
N1 reading is abstraction + stamina: essays on language, society, science philosophy; authors qualify, concede, and double-negate. The questions target stance, not facts.

Decoding the N1 essay voice

  • Double negation = assertion: 〜ないとは言えない／〜ないわけではない ≈ cautious yes. Mentally simplify every double negative as you read.
  • Concession before claim: 確かに〜。しかし〜。 The 確かに content is the opposing view; never select it as the author's opinion.
  • Rhetorical questions assert: 〜だろうか（いや、〜ない）. 本当にそうだろうか = "I think not."
  • Quoted view ≠ author's view: 〜という意見がある／〜と言われる introduces material the author will usually push against.

Question-type playbook

  • 下線部「それ」/「このこと」: resolve within ±2 sentences; substitute back. At N1, the referent can be an entire previous claim, not a noun.
  • 筆者の考え: weight the final paragraph + every post-しかし sentence. Eliminate options that are (a) the conceded view, (b) an example, (c) over-strong.
  • Compare-two: same as N2 method, but stances are subtler — often agreeing on facts while differing in evaluation (one sees risk, one sees opportunity).
  • 情報検索: still the easiest points on the paper. Do it before the long essay. Conditions at N1 add exception clauses — read every ※.
⚠ The "sounds smart" decoy N1 options paraphrase the passage's vocabulary into an elegant sentence that subtly inverts the logic (cause↔effect, necessary↔sufficient). Verify the direction of every claim against the text — matching keywords prove nothing.
💡 Stamina training: the combined session is 110 minutes of dense text. Once a week, do a full reading section in one sitting, no phone, exam posture. Concentration decay around minute 80 is a real, trainable variable.

n1-listening.title: N1 — Listening strategy
n1-listening.html:
Full natural speed, varied speakers (lectures, debates, interviews), and meaning carried by implicature: hedges, irony, half-finished sentences.

Hearing what isn't said

  • それはそうなんですが… = disagreement incoming; the speaker's real point follows.
  • 〜と言えなくもないけど = reluctant partial agreement, leaning no.
  • どうですかね／微妙ですね = skepticism. 前向きに検討します in business audio ≈ polite stall.
  • Praise + が = critique: よくできているとは思うんですが、ここがちょっと… The question will ask the speaker's evaluation — it's the part after が.

Format-specific notes

  • 概要理解 (lectures): the professor states a common assumption, then their research's twist. "最も言いたいこと" = the twist, almost always signposted by ところが／実は／むしろ.
  • 統合理解 2-question audio: question 1 is usually a specific choice, question 2 a stance/summary. Take the two-column notes from the N2 ritual but add a stance symbol (＋/−/？) per speaker.
  • 即時応答: stimulus lines now include keigo, idioms, and irony (「相変わらずだね」「ご無沙汰しております」「それを言っちゃおしまいだよ」). Drill volume is the only cure — these are 1-second pattern matches.
💡 Source match: N1 listening register = NHK radio news, ビジネス系 podcasts, university lecture clips, panel discussions. Variety of speakers matters more than total hours — the exam deliberately rotates voices, ages, and speech styles.

n1-tips.title: N1 — Tips & traps
n1-tips.html:
Score-moving tips

  1. Do the pass-mark arithmetic. 100/180 with a 19 floor means you choose where to be merely adequate. Typical winning shape: Language Knowledge 40+, Listening 35+, Reading 25+. Find YOUR shape from mocks and train the bottleneck.
  2. Grammar is the cheapest N1 section to max. The pattern list is finite (~120 forms); Kanzen Master N1 grammar twice through + attachment table ≈ 45–50/60 in Language Knowledge for most diligent takers.
  3. Read the 文章の文法 cloze passage as an essay first — its blanks test discourse logic (したがって vs それにもかかわらず), which you can only judge after grasping the flow.
  4. Old N1 ≠ new N1: pre-2010 1級 papers are useful drills but the current test is more reading-heavy and less obscure-vocab-heavy. Prioritize official post-2010 materials for calibration.

Trap gallery

⚠ Classical negative ん In 〜んばかり/〜んがため, ん is the classical negative-volitional, NOT contraction of ない or る: 言わんばかり = "as if to say" (positive sense). Options gloss it as negative — don't bite.
⚠ "Same meaning, wrong world" Many N1 pairs differ only in register/domain: 〜に即して (formal, rules/facts) vs 〜に沿って (neutral, plans); 〜を踏まえて (reports) vs 〜をもとに (creation). When two options "mean the same," the sentence's register decides.
⚠ Listening fatigue cliff 55 minutes at full speed, last session of a long day. The final 統合理解 block is where passes are lost. In training, always do listening LAST after a reading block, never fresh.
